Paper / Ink

Documentation

Production-minded docs for an approval-guardrail runtime.

This page is built from the repo as it exists today: Go API, Python SDK, decision workflows, deployment notes, operational constraints, and the production direction described by the codebase itself.

StartRuntimeDeployOperateSecurityReference

Runtime

Go API

Facts, invalidation, decisions, explanations, exports, auth, and org-level views.

SDK

Python client

Session-first integration surface plus OpenAI adapter and MCP server support.

Direction

Postgres + Redis

Local Badger stays for development. Shared-state production should not depend on one warm node.

Docs map

StartLocal quickstart and workflowRuntimeFacts, decisions, explanationsDeployProduction direction and envOperateHealth, limits, retentionSecurityHardening and threat modelReferenceAPI, SDK, repo map

Start

Use Velarix close to execution

The strongest integration path in this repo is not generic memory. It is approval integrity: capture facts, derive a recommendation, create a decision, and re-check before the action fires.

What the repo ships today

Go API, first-class decisions, session facts, invalidation, explanation endpoints, a Python SDK, a demo approval workflow, local Badger storage, and a production path centered on Postgres plus optional Redis coordination.

What it does not claim

This repo is not a finished finance-ops SaaS, not a generic memory platform, and not an audited compliance product. The docs below stay honest about current delivery versus production direction.

Step 01

Run the API locally

The maintained local path is Go plus Badger in dev mode. This is the fastest way to explore the approval-guardrail flow end to end.

export VELARIX_ENV=dev
export VELARIX_API_KEY=dev-admin-key
export VELARIX_BADGER_PATH="$(mktemp -d)"

go run main.go

Step 02

Record approval facts

Use the Python client to open a session, assert direct observations, derive the recommendation, and persist a decision against that fact graph.

from velarix.client import VelarixClient

client = VelarixClient(
    base_url="http://localhost:8080",
    api_key="dev-admin-key",
)

session = client.session("payment_approval_001")
session.observe("vendor_verified", {"vendor_id": "vendor-17"})
session.observe("invoice_approved", {"invoice_id": "inv-1042"})
session.observe("budget_available", {"cost_center": "ENG-01"})

session.derive(
    "decision.release_payment",
    [["vendor_verified", "invoice_approved", "budget_available"]],
    {"summary": "Payment can be released"},
)

Step 03

Check before executing

The product story is execution integrity. Call execute-check immediately before the side effect, then execute only if the decision is still valid.

decision = session.create_decision(
    "payment_release",
    fact_id="decision.release_payment",
    subject_ref="inv-1042",
    target_ref="vendor-17",
    dependency_fact_ids=[
        "vendor_verified",
        "invoice_approved",
        "budget_available",
    ],
)

check = session.execute_check(decision["decision_id"])
if check["executable"]:
    session.execute_decision(decision["decision_id"])

Runtime

Facts, decisions, and explanations

The repo models approval state as a fact graph. Decisions bind an action to that graph. Explanation surfaces are there so stale execution can be understood and debugged instead of merely denied.

Facts and derived facts

A fact can be asserted directly or derived from other facts through OR-of-AND justification sets. Root observations and derived conclusions live in the same session graph.

Decisions and dependency snapshots

A decision is created from a fact and stores the dependencies that justified the recommendation. That snapshot is checked again before execution.

Invalidation and pruning

When a root fact changes, downstream conclusions can collapse if they no longer have valid support. The theory and benchmark docs describe this as O(1) pruning through dominator-style ancestry checks.

Explanation and counterfactuals

The explain surfaces do more than dump state. They expose the chain of belief, blocked dependencies, and counterfactual reasoning about what would change if a fact disappeared.

Execution flow

1. Observe

Collect the approval facts that matter: vendor verification, invoice approval, policy sign-off, budget, access grant, or other hard prerequisites.

2. Derive

Create the derived recommendation that represents the action you want to take. This is where the graph becomes operational instead of descriptive.

3. Decide

Persist a first-class decision with subject references, targets, policy metadata, and dependency fact ids so execution is bound to a stable snapshot.

4. Re-check

Call execute-check immediately before money moves, access changes, or state mutates. If dependencies went stale, the action should stop here.

5. Explain

If a decision is blocked, inspect why-blocked, impact, explanation, history, and lineage. The repo treats operator visibility as part of the product, not an afterthought.

Deploy

Treat local and production paths differently

The codebase is explicit about this: Badger is the local adapter, not the product architecture. Production-like deployments should use Postgres, Redis, secure auth material, and a separately deployed frontend.

Local development

Badger is the local adapter. Use it for tests, demos, and fast iteration. It should not define the long-term production shape of the service.

Production direction

The target runtime is Postgres as the system of record, Redis for idempotency and rate limiting, and rebuildable in-memory engine caches instead of a permanently warm single node.

Hosted console path

The Next.js app in web/ points to the public API through NEXT_PUBLIC_VELARIX_API_URL. The private control-plane folder describes billing, provisioning, and infrastructure direction for Velarix Cloud.

Build

Containerize the API

The root Dockerfile currently builds the Go binary and starts it in lite mode by default. For production, run without --lite so the authenticated routes are mounted.

docker build -t velarix-engine .
docker run --rm -p 8080:8080 \
  -e VELARIX_ENV=prod \
  -e VELARIX_STORE_BACKEND=postgres \
  -e VELARIX_POSTGRES_DSN="postgres://..." \
  -e VELARIX_REDIS_URL="redis://..." \
  -e VELARIX_JWT_SECRET="replace-me" \
  -e VELARIX_ENCRYPTION_KEY="replace-me" \
  velarix-engine ./velarix

Frontend

Point the console at the API

The web app is a separate deployment unit. After your API is live, inject its public base URL into the Next.js frontend before building or deploying web/.

cd web
export NEXT_PUBLIC_VELARIX_API_URL="https://api.example.com"
npm run build

Cloud

Use the documented GCP path if you need managed infra

The repo already includes a deployment guide centered on Cloud Run, Cloud SQL, and Memorystore, with Vercel or a second Run service for the frontend.

gcloud run deploy velarix-api \
  --image gcr.io/$PROJECT_ID/velarix-engine:latest \
  --region us-central1 \
  --set-env-vars="VELARIX_ENV=prod" \
  --set-env-vars="VELARIX_STORE_BACKEND=postgres" \
  --set-env-vars="VELARIX_POSTGRES_DSN=postgres://..." \
  --set-env-vars="VELARIX_REDIS_URL=redis://..."

Required configuration

VariableValueWhy it matters
VELARIX_ENVdev | prodControls production-only safeguards such as strict encryption requirements and secure cookies.
VELARIX_STORE_BACKENDbadger | postgresSelects the persistence strategy. Postgres is the intended production direction.
VELARIX_POSTGRES_DSNpostgres://...Required when using the Postgres-backed runtime store.
VELARIX_REDIS_URLredis://...Used for the shared-store production path for rate limiting and coordination.
VELARIX_JWT_SECRETsecret stringRequired for secure console auth outside local development.
VELARIX_ENCRYPTION_KEY32-byte keyRequired outside dev when encryption at rest is expected.
VELARIX_ALLOWED_ORIGINScomma-separated originsControls browser access for the console and other web clients.
VELARIX_API_KEYadmin or service keyUsed in local flows and service-to-service access patterns.
NEXT_PUBLIC_VELARIX_API_URLhttps://api.example.comBuild-time API target for the Next.js console in web/.

Operate

Observe health, pressure, and failure modes

The repo already documents the operational contract. Health endpoints, retry behavior, backpressure headers, and retention rules matter as much as the fact model once the service sits in front of a real execution system.

Health endpoints

GET /health is the basic liveness check. GET /health/full exposes deeper health information for admin and operator use.

Backpressure and retries

Write saturation returns 503 with Retry-After: 1 and X-Velarix-Backpressure: 1. The repo expects clients to retry safely with idempotency keys.

Rate limiting

Local and shared-store paths exist today, but the long-term production shape should be Redis-backed rather than tied to local process assumptions.

Retention and recovery

Badger backup and restore exist for the local adapter. The production story should center on Postgres backup strategy, Redis recovery, and artifact handling for exports and snapshots.

Operator-facing errors

StatusMeaningExpected response
409 ConflictStale decision executionThe key approval-guardrail failure mode. Inspect reason_codes, blocked_by, and why-blocked before retrying.
429 Too Many RequestsRate limit exceededHonor Retry-After and resend with the same idempotency key where appropriate.
503 Service UnavailablePer-org write backpressureSignals burst protection. Retry with the same idempotency key once pressure clears.
401 / 403Auth or scope failureUsually caused by missing API keys, invalid JWTs, cross-org access, or insufficient route scope.
400 / 404Bad payload or missing factCommon when justification sets are malformed, facts are absent, or a decision points to missing dependencies.

Security

Secure the approval path, not just the API surface

Velarix is valuable when it prevents stale, high-impact actions from executing. The repo's security posture is therefore tied to provenance, org boundaries, key handling, and fresh execute-checks.

What exists today

API key auth, JWT user auth, org-scoped request handling, append-only history with verification hashes, scope-aware route checks, and optional Badger encryption at rest.

Primary threats

Cross-tenant access, stolen service credentials, stale decision execution, history tampering, and denial of service from bursty writers are the core threats modeled in the repo.

Practical guidance

If the workflow can move money, change access, or create audit exposure, require a fresh execute-check immediately before the business action.

Honest gaps

Password reset is not a finished production delivery flow, shared-store defaults still need to replace local assumptions, and export hashes are not compliance evidence.

Hardening rule

If a workflow can move money, change access, or create audit exposure, require a fresh execute-check immediately before the final action.

Reference

Map the API surface back to the repo

The current codebase exposes more routes than the short marketing docs suggest. The groups below reflect the actual server surface and the SDK entry points you would care about after deployment.

Sessions and facts

RouteDescriptionSource
POST /v1/s/{session_id}/factsAssert root or derived facts into a session.api/server.go
POST /v1/s/{session_id}/facts/{id}/invalidateInvalidate a root fact and collapse stale descendants.api/server.go
GET /v1/s/{session_id}/facts/{id}Fetch a fact directly.api/server.go
GET /v1/s/{session_id}/graphReturn the session graph used by the dashboard explorer.api/server.go
GET /v1/s/{session_id}/historyReturn append-only session history.api/server.go
GET /v1/s/{session_id}/sliceRender the current valid slice as JSON or markdown.api/server.go

Decisions and execution

RouteDescriptionSource
POST /v1/s/{session_id}/decisionsCreate a first-class decision from a fact or dependency set.api/server.go
POST /v1/s/{session_id}/decisions/{decision_id}/recomputeRefresh dependency state and recompute status.api/server.go
POST /v1/s/{session_id}/decisions/{decision_id}/execute-checkAsk whether the decision is still executable.api/server.go
POST /v1/s/{session_id}/decisions/{decision_id}/executeAttempt execution and block if dependencies are stale.api/server.go
GET /v1/s/{session_id}/decisions/{decision_id}/why-blockedExplain the blocking facts for a stale decision.api/server.go
GET /v1/s/{session_id}/decisions/{decision_id}/lineageInspect upstream and downstream decision lineage.api/server.go

Explanation, export, and org views

RouteDescriptionSource
GET /v1/s/{session_id}/explainReturn reasoning and counterfactual explanation for a fact.api/server.go
GET /v1/s/{session_id}/facts/{id}/impactEstimate downstream impact if the fact changes.api/server.go
GET /v1/s/{session_id}/exportExport session state directly.api/server.go
POST /v1/s/{session_id}/export-jobsCreate an export job for download workflows.api/server.go
GET /v1/org/decisionsList org-level decision records.api/server.go
GET /v1/org/usageReturn usage counters for the current org.api/server.go

Auth, keys, and console services

RouteDescriptionSource
POST /v1/auth/registerCreate a console account.api/server.go
POST /v1/auth/loginIssue a JWT-backed console session.api/server.go
POST /v1/auth/reset-requestBegin password reset flow.api/server.go
GET /v1/keysList generated service keys.api/server.go
POST /v1/keys/generateCreate a new API key.api/server.go
DELETE /v1/keys/{key}Revoke a key.api/server.go

SDK and integration surfaces

SurfaceLayerWhy it matters
VelarixClient / session()Python SDKPrimary entry point for session-scoped operations in sdks/python/velarix/client.py.
observe / derive / invalidateFact lifecycleAssert direct observations, derived facts, and invalidations against the session graph.
create_decision / execute_check / execute_decisionExecution integrityThe core approval-guardrail path recommended by docs/INTEGRATION_GUIDE.md.
adapters.openai.OpenAIModel-provider surfaceOpenAI-compatible adapter for feeding observations and explanations through the same runtime.
mcp_server.pyMCP integrationExposes assert_fact, get_fact, explain_reasoning, and a session context resource for MCP clients.

Canonical files to read

FileRoleWhat to inspect
main.goRuntime bootstrapSelects lite mode, storage backend, encryption checks, and server startup behavior.
api/server.goPublic HTTP surfaceMounts the /v1 routes for sessions, org views, auth, billing, and decision workflows.
api/decision_contracts.goProduct wedgeCanonical decision and approval-integrity flow according to docs/README.md.
demo/approval_integrity.pyMaintained demoShows decision creation, stale invalidation, blocked execution, and explanation.
tests/e2e_test.goEnd-to-end behaviorUseful anchor when validating actual repo behavior beyond the docs and demo.
View repositoryOpen console
